_work.title: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
_work.creator: various
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The visual record of the Neoplatonic tradition, from the late-antique masters Plotinus, Porphyry, Iamblichus and Proclus through Boethius, Synesius and the Pseudo-Dionysian celestial hierarchies to the Florentine revival of Ficino, Pico, Plethon and Bessarion: portraits of the philosophers, manuscripts of the Timaeus, the Enneads and the Liber de causis, the Tablet of Cebes, Plato's Cave, and the concentric cosmos of spheres through which the One emanates and the soul returns.

entries.title: Marble Portrait Bust of an Ancient Philosopher
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Anonymous (Greek/Roman)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons. (CC BY-SA 4.0, photo Zde)
entries.summary:
A Hellenistic marble portrait of a bearded philosopher from Delphi, of the contemplative type by which the ancient world imagined its sages — the very kind of image evoked for Plotinus (c. 204/5–270 CE), founder of Neoplatonism, who notoriously refused to sit for a portrait.

## Iconography
The heavily bearded head turns slightly, the brow furrowed in thought, eyes deep-set beneath a high forehead — the canonical Greek 'philosopher type'. The surviving marble is a Roman-era image of an unidentified thinker (museum label: 'maybe Plutarch').

## Significance
Plotinus taught that all reality flows by [[motif:emanation|emanation]] from a single ineffable source, the [[motif:the one|One]], through Intellect (Nous) and the [[motif:world soul|World-Soul]], and that the philosopher's task is the soul's return to that source. His pupil Porphyry records that Plotinus disdained the body so far that he would not have his likeness made — making every 'portrait of Plotinus' necessarily a borrowed contemplative type like this one. Such busts furnished the Renaissance with its image of the divinely-inspired sage.

## For artists & game designers
The default 'ancient philosopher' face: full beard, lined brow, inward gaze — read instantly as wisdom and asceticism.

entries.title: Averroes and Porphyry in Disputation (manuscript miniature)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Monfredo de Monte Imperiali
entries.summary:
A medieval manuscript miniature showing the Neoplatonist Porphyry (c. 234–305 CE) in imagined disputation with the Andalusian philosopher Averroes — an anachronistic pairing that registers Porphyry's central place in the scholastic curriculum.

## Iconography
Two robed scholars are seated face to face in gesture of debate, labelled with their names; the miniature comes from a herbal/philosophical compilation by Monfredo de Monte Imperiali.

## Significance
Porphyry was Plotinus's pupil, editor of the *Enneads*, and author of the *Isagoge* — the introduction to Aristotle's logic that became the standard textbook of the medieval West and the seed of the universals controversy. As a Neoplatonist he transmitted the doctrine of [[motif:emanation|emanation]] and the soul's ascent, while his anti-Christian polemic made him notorious. The medieval image of the disputing philosophers shows how late-antique Platonism survived embedded in the logic curriculum.

## For artists & game designers
A clean visual shorthand for 'the scholarly disputation': two seated, gesturing figures with name-labels.

citations.text: Porphyry, *Isagoge*; ed. and trans. J. Barnes (2003).

entries.title: Iamblichus, De mysteriis Aegyptiorum (Ficino's Latin edition)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Iamblichus; trans. Marsilio Ficino
entries.summary:
A page from the Renaissance Latin edition of Iamblichus's *De mysteriis Aegyptiorum* (On the Mysteries of the Egyptians) in the translation by Marsilio Ficino, the foundational text of Neoplatonic theurgy.

## Iconography
A printed Latin page in humanist roman type, the running title naming the *Mysteria Aegyptiorum*, from the bundle of Neoplatonic translations Ficino issued through the Aldine press.

## Significance
Iamblichus of Chalcis (c. 245–325 CE) transformed Plotinian Neoplatonism by insisting that the soul ascends not by contemplation alone but through [[motif:theurgy|theurgy]] — ritual acts that draw down divine power. His *De mysteriis* defends Egyptian and Chaldean rites against Porphyry's skepticism and elaborates the procession of gods, henads and daemons through which the [[motif:the one|One]] is approached. Ficino's translation made theurgic Neoplatonism available to the Florentine Academy and to the whole Renaissance magical tradition.

## For artists & game designers
Reference for the 'rediscovered occult treatise': dense humanist print, Latin headings, the look of a magus's working library.

citations.text: Iamblichus, *On the Mysteries*, trans. Clarke, Dillon & Hershbell (2003).

entries.title: Synesius of Cyrene, Letters (Greek manuscript)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Synesius of Cyrene
entries.date: manuscript (Byzantine copy of a c. 400 CE text)
entries.summary:
A page of the Greek manuscript (Paris, BnF, Gr. 1040) preserving the *Letters* of Synesius of Cyrene (c. 373–414 CE), the Neoplatonist student of Hypatia who became a Christian bishop.

## Iconography
A column of Byzantine Greek minuscule on parchment, the text of Synesius's celebrated correspondence.

## Significance
Synesius studied at Alexandria under [[motif:hypatia|Hypatia]], to whom several of these letters are addressed, and fused Plotinian Neoplatonism with Christianity — his *Hymns* and treatise *On Dreams* describe the soul's ascent through the celestial spheres and the World-Soul. As Bishop of Ptolemais he embodies the late-antique passage of pagan Platonic philosophy into Christian thought. His letters are a primary source for the intellectual world of Hypatia's Alexandria.

## For artists & game designers
Authentic look of a Byzantine Greek letter-collection — useful as a 'lost correspondence' prop.

entries.title: Hypatia of Alexandria (detail from Raphael's School of Athens)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Raphael (Raffaello Sanzio)
entries.repository: Stanza della Segnatura, Apostolic Palace, Vatican
entries.summary:
A detail from Raphael's fresco *The School of Athens* showing the white-robed figure traditionally identified as Hypatia of Alexandria, the only woman among the assembled philosophers.

## Iconography
A young figure in pale robes gazes out toward the viewer from the left foreground of the great architectural hall of philosophers, set apart by colour and direct gaze.

## Significance
Hypatia (c. 350/370–415 CE) was the Neoplatonist mathematician and philosopher who headed the Platonic school at Alexandria, teaching Plotinus and commenting on Diophantus and Apollonius. Lynched by a Christian mob in 415, she became the emblem of pagan learning destroyed by fanaticism. Raphael's inclusion of her — facing outward at the threshold of the [[motif:school of athens|School of Athens]] — canonised her within the Renaissance pantheon of sages.

## For artists & game designers
The archetypal 'woman scholar among men': singled out by light robes and an outward gaze that breaks the scene.

entries.title: Boethius and Lady Philosophy (Consolation manuscript)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Anonymous illuminator
entries.summary:
A medieval manuscript miniature of Boethius (c. 477–524 CE) visited in his cell by the personified Lady Philosophy, the central scene of the *Consolation of Philosophy*.

## Iconography
The imprisoned philosopher sits while a crowned woman — Philosophia, sometimes shown with a ladder of letters on her gown rising from Pi to Theta — leans toward him in instruction.

## Significance
Boethius wrote the *Consolatio* awaiting execution; in it Lady [[motif:lady philosophy|Philosophy]] leads him from grief to the vision of the supreme [[motif:the good|Good]], drawing on Plato, Plotinus and Proclus to argue that all things turn back toward the One and that Fortune's gifts are nothing beside divine providence. The work transmitted Neoplatonic cosmology to the entire Latin Middle Ages and was among its most copied and translated books.

## For artists & game designers
The model 'wisdom-figure visits the prisoner' tableau: crowned female mentor, despairing seated man, a cell.

citations.text: Boethius, *The Consolation of Philosophy*, trans. V. Watts (1969).

entries.title: The Wheel of Fortune and Boethius on his Sickbed (De consolatione philosophiae)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Anonymous illuminator
entries.summary:
A miniature from a manuscript of Boethius's *De consolatione philosophiae* (Utrecht, UB Hs. 1335) pairing the Wheel of Fortune with the ailing Boethius on his sickbed.

## Iconography
Fortuna turns a great wheel on which crowned figures rise to the top (regno — 'I reign') and are flung down (regnavi — 'I have reigned'); alongside, Boethius lies stricken on his bed.

## Significance
The [[motif:rota fortunae|Wheel of Fortune]] is the *Consolation*'s most enduring image: Philosophy teaches that worldly goods spin on Fortune's wheel and are therefore worthless beside the changeless [[motif:the one|One]] that Neoplatonism sets above all becoming. The motif, fusing Stoic and Platonic providence, dominated medieval art and literature from Carmina Burana to the cathedral rose windows.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'rise and fall' device: a turning wheel with figures cresting and tumbling, labelled by station.

citations.text: H. R. Patch, *The Goddess Fortuna in Mediaeval Literature* (1927).

entries.title: Marble Bust of Plato (Roman copy)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman copy after a Greek original)
entries.date: 1st century CE copy of a c. 350–340 BCE original
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons. (CC BY-SA 4.0, photo O. S. M. Amin)
entries.summary:
A Roman marble bust of Plato (428/7–348/7 BCE), copied from a Greek original made within the philosopher's own lifetime — the source of the canonical Platonic likeness.

## Iconography
A dignified bearded head with full, swept hair and calm features, in the idealising portrait mode of a fourth-century master.

## Significance
Plato is the fountainhead of the whole Neoplatonic tradition. The doctrines the Neoplatonists systematised — the [[motif:the forms|Forms]], the [[motif:the good|Good]] as the sun of the intelligible realm in the *Republic*, the demiurge and World-Soul of the *Timaeus*, the soul's ascent in the *Symposium* and *Phaedrus* — all descend from his dialogues. Plotinus, Proclus and Ficino read themselves as his faithful interpreters; this bust is the face that the Academy gave the Western imagination.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical Plato head — broad brow, full beard, serene gaze — instantly legible as 'the founding philosopher'.

citations.text: G. M. A. Richter, *The Portraits of the Greeks* (1965).

entries.title: Plato and Aristotle (detail from Raphael's School of Athens)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Raphael (Raffaello Sanzio)
entries.repository: Stanza della Segnatura, Apostolic Palace, Vatican
entries.summary:
The central detail of Raphael's *School of Athens*: Plato and Aristotle stride forward side by side, Plato pointing upward to the heavens, Aristotle gesturing toward the earth.

## Iconography
Plato (modelled on Leonardo) holds his *Timaeus* and raises a finger to the sky; Aristotle holds his *Ethics* and extends a level hand toward the ground — the opposed gestures of transcendence and immanence.

## Significance
The pairing crystallises the Renaissance reading of philosophy: Plato the metaphysician of the eternal [[motif:the forms|Forms]] above, Aristotle the analyst of the sensible world below. By placing the *[[motif:timaeus|Timaeus]]* in Plato's hand, Raphael flags the dialogue that the Neoplatonists read as the key to cosmic [[motif:emanation|emanation]] and the [[motif:world soul|World-Soul]]. The image, painted at the height of Ficino's influence, is the visual manifesto of Florentine Platonism.

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate 'two masters, two worldviews' composition: one points up to heaven, the other down to earth.

citations.text: I. Hart, ‘Raphael's School of Athens', *Renaissance Studies* (2010).

entries.title: Plato's Academy (Roman mosaic, Pompeii)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman mosaicist)
entries.place: Pompeii (Villa of T. Siminius Stephanus); now Naples
entries.summary:
A Roman floor mosaic from Pompeii depicting a circle of philosophers — traditionally read as Plato and members of his Academy — gathered in learned conversation beneath a tree.

## Iconography
Seven draped figures sit and stand around a central sage who points with a rod at a celestial globe; a sundial sits atop a column and a walled city rises behind, framing the scene of philosophical instruction.

## Significance
The Academy that Plato founded in the grove of Akademos became, through its Neoplatonic successors at Athens and Alexandria, the institutional home of the tradition until Justinian closed it in 529 CE. This mosaic is among the very few ancient images of philosophical schooling, showing the cosmological teaching — the globe, the sundial — that fed the Neoplatonic concern with the order of the [[motif:concentric spheres|heavens]] and the soul's place within it.

## For artists & game designers
Reference for the 'open-air philosophy school': a seated circle, a pointing teacher, a globe and column under a tree.

entries.title: Plato's Timaeus in Calcidius's Latin Translation (manuscript)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Calcidius (translator/commentator)
entries.summary:
A medieval manuscript page of Plato's *Timaeus* in the partial Latin translation and commentary of Calcidius (4th century CE) — for centuries the only Plato the Latin West possessed.

## Iconography
A two-column Latin manuscript page of the *Timaeus* text with Calcidius's commentary, sometimes accompanied by geometric and astronomical diagrams of the cosmic soul.

## Significance
The *[[motif:timaeus|Timaeus]]* is Plato's account of how the divine craftsman (demiurge) fashions the cosmos by imposing mathematical harmony on chaos and animates it with a [[motif:world soul|World-Soul]] compounded from the Same and the Different. Through Calcidius this dialogue shaped the entire medieval Platonic cosmology — the school of Chartres, the macrocosm–microcosm analogy, the music of the spheres — and supplied the Neoplatonists their model of orderly [[motif:emanation|emanation]] from the One.

## For artists & game designers
The 'cosmogony codex': dense Latin columns and circle-diagrams of how the world was made.

citations.text: Calcidius, *On Plato's Timaeus*, ed. and trans. J. Magee (2016).

entries.title: Plato's Cave
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Jan Saenredam after Cornelis Cornelisz van Haarlem
entries.summary:
Jan Saenredam's 1604 engraving after Cornelis van Haarlem, the most famous visual rendering of Plato's Allegory of the Cave from Book VII of the *Republic*.

## Iconography
In a vast cavern, chained prisoners stare at shadows cast on the wall by figures and objects paraded before a fire; at the cave's mouth daylight and a freed figure ascending mark the world of reality beyond illusion.

## Significance
The [[motif:allegory of the cave|Allegory of the Cave]] dramatises the Platonic and Neoplatonic doctrine of ascent: the soul, bound to the shadow-world of the senses, must turn (periagoge) and climb toward the light of the [[motif:the good|Good]], figured as the [[motif:the sun|Sun]] outside the cave. For the Neoplatonists this was the very pattern of the soul's return to the One. Saenredam's print, with its Latin verses on truth and appearance, made the parable a staple of early-modern visual philosophy.

## For artists & game designers
The template 'illusion vs. reality' scene: chained watchers, firelit shadows, a blinding exit to daylight.

entries.title: Liber de Causis (manuscript, Venice Lat. 288)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Anonymous (after Proclus); 'Pseudo-Aristotle'
entries.date: medieval manuscript (text 9th-century Arabic adaptation of Proclus)
entries.summary:
A manuscript opening (Venice, Lat. 288, fol. 2r) of the *Liber de Causis* — the 'Book of Causes', the Neoplatonic treatise that medieval Europe long attributed to Aristotle.

## Iconography
A Latin manuscript page with rubricated incipit, opening the chain of propositions on the First Cause.

## Significance
The *[[motif:liber de causis|Liber de Causis]]* is an Arabic adaptation of Proclus's *Elements of Theology*, translated into Latin in the 12th century and read as Aristotle's crowning metaphysics until Aquinas, comparing it with the newly available Proclus, exposed its true Neoplatonic source. Its propositions on how all multiplicity proceeds from the [[motif:the one|First Cause]] by [[motif:emanation|emanation]] carried the core of Proclean Neoplatonism into scholastic theology, shaping Albert the Great, Aquinas and the doctrine of being itself.

## For artists & game designers
A 'misattributed grimoire of metaphysics' prop: austere Latin propositions, a rubricated opening, hidden Greek origins.

citations.text: *Liber de Causis*, trans. D. J. Brand (1984).

entries.title: Saints Dionysius the Areopagite and Hierotheos (icon)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Anonymous (Byzantine/post-Byzantine icon painter)
entries.repository: Bank of Cyprus Cultural Foundation, Nicosia
entries.summary:
A painted icon of Saints Dionysius the Areopagite and Hierotheos, the figures to whom the foundational corpus of Christian Neoplatonic mysticism was ascribed.

## Iconography
Two haloed bishop-saints stand in liturgical vestments, bearing gospel books — the standard hieratic icon type for the holy hierarchs.

## Significance
The writings of [[motif:dionysius the areopagite|Pseudo-Dionysius]] (c. 500 CE) — *On the Celestial Hierarchy*, *On the Divine Names*, *Mystical Theology* — recast Proclus's Neoplatonism in Christian terms, describing the [[motif:celestial hierarchy|nine angelic orders]] arranged in three triads through which divine light descends and creation returns to God. Transmitted under the name of Paul's Athenian convert, the corpus became the charter of medieval angelology and apophatic mysticism, shaping Eriugena, Aquinas and Dante's heaven. Hierotheos is named in the corpus as Dionysius's teacher.

## For artists & game designers
The 'holy hierarch' icon type — twin haloed bishops with gospel books — and the conceptual root of the nine-tier angelic hierarchy.

citations.text: Pseudo-Dionysius, *The Complete Works*, trans. C. Luibheid (1987).

entries.title: Diagram of Planetary Motion (Macrobian cosmology, after Somnium Scipionis tradition)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.date: diagram (after the medieval Macrobian / Somnium Scipionis tradition)
entries.summary:
A schematic diagram of planetary motion in the geocentric cosmos, in the tradition of the spheres-and-orbits figures that illustrated Macrobius's *Commentary on the Dream of Scipio*.

## Iconography
The paths of the planets are traced across a field representing the ordered celestial spheres turning about a central Earth — the diagrammatic form of cosmic order through time.

## Significance
Macrobius's *Commentarii in Somnium Scipionis* (early 5th century) was, with Calcidius's *Timaeus*, the great medieval handbook of Neoplatonic cosmology. Expounding Cicero's vision of the soul ascending through the planetary [[motif:concentric spheres|spheres]] to its celestial home, Macrobius taught the descent of the soul through the spheres at birth and its return at death, and the [[motif:harmony of the spheres|harmony of the spheres]]. Diagrams of this kind made the Neoplatonic ordered cosmos visible to medieval and Renaissance readers.

## For artists & game designers
A clean orrery-style schematic of nested planetary orbits — the geometry of the soul's celestial journey.

citations.text: Macrobius, *Commentary on the Dream of Scipio*, trans. W. H. Stahl (1952).

key_concepts: descent and return of the soul

entries.title: The Ptolemaic (Geocentric) System of Concentric Spheres (Cellarius, Harmonia Macrocosmica)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Andreas Cellarius; engraved by Jan van Loon
entries.summary:
Andreas Cellarius's spectacular hand-coloured plate of the Ptolemaic geocentric universe from the *Harmonia Macrocosmica* (1660), showing the Earth ringed by the nested celestial spheres.

## Iconography
Concentric rings carry the Moon, Sun, planets and the fixed stars around a central Earth, the whole enclosed by the outermost heavens and crowned by the [[motif:the empyrean|Empyrean]]; cherubs and astronomers occupy the corners.

## Significance
This is the closest standard image to the Neoplatonic [[motif:concentric spheres|cosmos as interlocking circles]]: the diagram of nested spheres through which the Neoplatonists imagined divine power radiating outward from the centre (or, inversely, the soul ascending ring by ring back toward the One). The same scheme of graded, concentric heavens underlies the [[motif:celestial hierarchy|Dionysian hierarchies]], Dante's *Paradiso*, and the macrocosm of Renaissance Platonism — the visible architecture of [[motif:emanation|emanation]] and return.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'nested-spheres cosmos' diagram — a ringed universe map ready to repurpose as a cosmological wheel or skill tree.

citations.text: A. Cellarius, *Harmonia Macrocosmica* (1660); facsimile R. van Gent (2006).

entries.title: The Anima Mundi (World-Soul) — Fludd, Utriusque Cosmi Historia
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Robert Fludd; engraver Matthäus Merian
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An engraving from Robert Fludd's *Utriusque Cosmi Historia* (1617–21) depicting the structure of the macrocosm and the [[motif:world soul|Anima Mundi]] that binds it — Neoplatonic cosmology rendered as a Hermetic diagram.

## Iconography
Concentric bands of the created cosmos descend from the divine source through the angelic, celestial and elemental realms; in Fludd's famous related plate a nude female figure of Nature, chained at the wrist to the cloud of God above and leading an ape of art below, personifies the soul that animates the whole. [CHECK-RIGHTS]

## Significance
Fludd's encyclopaedia is the high-water mark of Renaissance Neoplatonic-Hermetic cosmology: the universe is a graded [[motif:chain of being|chain of being]] through which the One [[motif:emanation|emanates]], unified by the [[motif:anima mundi|World-Soul]] that Plato's *Timaeus* and Plotinus had set between the eternal and the corporeal. His system, drawing on Ficino and the *Corpus Hermeticum*, provoked the famous polemic with Kepler over whether the cosmos is to be read by mystical analogy or by mathematics.

## For artists & game designers
The complete 'great chain' cosmogram: banded realms from God to matter, joined by a chain — a ready-made cosmology screen.

entries.title: The Monad (point within a circle)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Anonymous (modern vector after a classical symbol)
entries.date: modern rendering of a classical symbol
entries.summary:
The classical sign of the Monad — a single point enclosed in a circle — the simplest emblem of the Neoplatonic First Principle.

## Iconography
A dot at the centre of a perfect circle: the point is the undivided source, the circle its boundless extension.

## Significance
The [[motif:monad|Monad]] (Greek *monas*, 'unity') is the Pythagorean and Neoplatonic figure of the [[motif:the one|One]] — the unbegotten source from which all number, and so all reality, proceeds by [[motif:emanation|emanation]] and to which it returns. For Plotinus the One is beyond being and beyond predication; the point-in-circle gives that ineffable source its minimal visible form, the seed of every later diagram of concentric procession. The same sign recurs in Neopythagorean number theory and in Hermetic and alchemical symbolism (echoed in Dee's *Monas Hieroglyphica*).

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate minimal cosmological glyph — a centred point in a ring, instantly reading as 'origin / unity / source'.

citations.text: Plotinus, *Enneads* V.1, ‘On the Three Primary Hypostases'.

entries.title: Tabula Cebetis (The Tablet of Cebes)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
A painted *Tabula Cebetis* (Rijksmuseum SK-A-2372), the allegorical 'Tablet of Cebes' that maps the whole course of human life as a journey through walled enclosures toward true happiness.

## Iconography
Concentric ringed walls enclose successive precincts of life: at the gate a Genius instructs entering souls, Deceit offers her cup, Fortune turns on her ball, false opinions and pleasures crowd the lower courts, while a steep path leads up past True Education to the crowning citadel of Felicity. [CHECK-RIGHTS: image is PD]

## Significance
The *[[motif:tabula cebetis|Tablet of Cebes]]*, a Greek dialogue of the 1st century CE pseudonymously ascribed to Socrates' pupil Cebes, was hugely popular in the Renaissance as a moral-philosophical allegory of the soul's ascent. Its diagram of [[motif:concentric enclosures|concentric enclosures]] climbing toward the Good visually echoes the Neoplatonic ascent through graded realms, and it was repeatedly printed and painted from Holbein onward as a meditation on virtue, fortune and true education.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made allegorical level-map: ringed courts of vice and illusion climbing to a summit of true happiness.

citations.text: *The Tabula of Cebes*, ed. and trans. J. T. Fitzgerald & L. M. White (1983).

entries.title: Marsilio Ficino (with Landino, Poliziano and Chalkondyles), Ghirlandaio fresco detail
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Domenico Ghirlandaio
entries.repository: Tornabuoni Chapel, Santa Maria Novella, Florence
entries.summary:
A detail from Ghirlandaio's fresco *The Angel Appearing to Zacharias* in Santa Maria Novella, showing a group of Florentine humanists traditionally identified as Marsilio Ficino, Cristoforo Landino, Angelo Poliziano and Demetrios Chalkondyles.

## Iconography
Four scholars in contemporary dress stand conversing at the edge of the sacred scene — the donor-portrait convention by which Ghirlandaio inserted the living intellectual elite of Medicean Florence into a biblical narrative.

## Significance
Marsilio Ficino (1433–1499) was the architect of the Renaissance Neoplatonic revival: at the [[motif:florentine academy|Platonic Academy]] under Medici patronage he translated the complete Plato, Plotinus and the *Corpus Hermeticum* into Latin and wrote the *Theologia Platonica* and *De vita*, fusing Platonic [[motif:emanation|emanation]] with Christian theology and astral magic. This group portrait places him among the philological and philosophical circle through which his recovered Platonism reshaped European thought.

## For artists & game designers
The 'circle of scholars' cameo — contemporary intellectuals slipped into a sacred scene as living portraits.

citations.text: M. J. B. Allen & V. Rees (eds.), *Marsilio Ficino: His Theology, His Philosophy* (2002).

entries.title: Portrait of Pico della Mirandola (Rubens)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Peter Paul Rubens (after an earlier likeness)
entries.summary:
A portrait of Giovanni Pico della Mirandola (1463–1494), attributed to Rubens after an earlier likeness, preserving the image of the prodigy of the Florentine Academy.

## Iconography
A young man with long flowing hair, in three-quarter view, in the grave humanist portrait manner — the conventional image of the brilliant philosopher cut off young.

## Significance
Pico, the friend of Ficino, sought a *concordia* of all philosophies — Plato, Aristotle, the Hermetica, and above all the [[motif:christian kabbalah|Kabbalah]], which he was the first Christian to mine for Neoplatonic and apologetic ends. His *Oration on the Dignity of Man* and the 900 *Conclusiones* (1486), partly condemned by the Church, frame the human soul as a free microcosm able to ascend the [[motif:chain of being|chain of being]] toward the divine. With Ficino he made Florence the centre of Neoplatonic and Hermetic-Kabbalistic synthesis.

## For artists & game designers
The 'young genius' portrait — long hair, calm face, scholar's robe — the prodigy who died at thirty-one.

citations.text: S. A. Farmer, *Syncretism in the West: Pico's 900 Theses* (1998).

key_concepts: Oration on the Dignity of Man

entries.title: Gemistos Plethon (detail from Gozzoli's Procession of the Magi)
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Benozzo Gozzoli
entries.repository: Magi Chapel, Palazzo Medici Riccardi, Florence
entries.summary:
A figure from Benozzo Gozzoli's *Procession of the Magi* in the Medici chapel, identified as the Byzantine philosopher Georgios Gemistos Plethon (c. 1355/60–1452/4).

## Iconography
An elderly bearded sage in rich robes rides amid the magnificent cavalcade of the Magi that winds through a Tuscan landscape — a contemporary scholar woven into the sacred procession.

## Significance
Plethon came to the Council of Ferrara–Florence (1438–39) and there, by lecturing on Plato and the differences between Plato and Aristotle, ignited the Florentine Platonic revival — it was reportedly his teaching that moved Cosimo de' Medici to found the [[motif:florentine academy|Platonic Academy]] under Ficino. A radical Neoplatonist who in his *Nomoi* proposed reviving a reformed pagan polytheism organised around the [[motif:the one|One]] and a hierarchy of gods, Plethon is the hinge between late Byzantine and Renaissance Neoplatonism. Bessarion was his pupil.

## For artists & game designers
The 'foreign sage in the procession' — an exotic bearded philosopher riding among Renaissance nobility.

citations.text: C. M. Woodhouse, *George Gemistos Plethon: The Last of the Hellenes* (1986).

entries.title: Portrait of Cardinal Bessarion
entries.work: Neoplatonism: The One, Emanation, and the World-Soul
entries.creator: Justus van Gent / Pedro Berruguete
entries.summary:
A portrait of Cardinal Basilios Bessarion (1403–1472) from the series of 'famous men' painted for the studiolo of the Duke of Urbino.

## Iconography
The bearded prelate is shown in three-quarter view in cardinal's robes, one of the uomini illustri assembled to embody learning and virtue.

## Significance
Bessarion, pupil of Plethon, was the Byzantine churchman who, after the Council of Florence, became a Roman cardinal and the great patron of Greek learning in Italy. His *In Calumniatorem Platonis* (1469) defended Plato against George of Trebizond's attacks and tempered Plethon's pagan Platonism into a form acceptable to Christianity, advancing the Neoplatonic [[motif:the one|One]] within orthodox bounds. His vast manuscript library — bequeathed to Venice as the seed of the Biblioteca Marciana — preserved much of the Greek Neoplatonic corpus for the West.

## For artists & game designers
The 'scholar-prelate' portrait — bearded, robed in cardinal red, dignified — the bridge between Byzantium and the Latin West.
